skill.name: twscrape
skill.description: Documentation for using twscrape to collect Twitter/X data with async GraphQL scraping, account rotation, sessions, proxies, and exports.
skill.summary: Use twscrape to collect Twitter/X tweets, profiles, followers, trends, and raw responses with async workflows.
skill.author: 2025Emma

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution matches are mostly false positives from fenced documentation examples and inline backticks, not executable skill code. However, the skill intentionally documents unofficial Twitter/X scraping, account rotation to bypass API limitations, credential and cookie handling, proxy use, and local export of scraped data. No prompt injection or confirmed malware was found, but the abuse and compliance risk is high for marketplace publication.

high_findings.title: Unofficial Scraping and Rate Limit Bypass Guidance
high_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill explicitly recommends Twitter/X scraping, alternatives to the official API, account rotation, and rate-limit handling. This can enable platform abuse, policy violations, and large-scale collection without clear consent controls.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The guidance is explicit and repeated in the skill overview and rate-limit sections. It is legitimate for some research uses, but the bypass and rotation language creates strong abuse risk.

high_findings.title: Credential, Cookie, and Email Password Handling
high_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The examples show adding Twitter/X account passwords, email addresses, email passwords, and cookies to an account pool. These values are sensitive and may be stored in the local accounts database or exposed through examples and logs.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sensitive fields are shown directly in account setup examples. The examples use placeholders, but they teach workflows that handle real credentials and cookies.

medium_findings.title: Proxy and SOCKS Configuration for Scraping Traffic
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill documents HTTP proxies, SOCKS proxies, proxy credentials, and dynamic proxy changes. Proxies can be valid for access control, but they also increase attribution, credential exposure, and abuse risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The proxy strings and SOCKS examples are concrete and directly related to network scraping. Legitimate use is possible, so this is medium rather than critical.

medium_findings.title: User-Executed CLI and Package Installation Commands
medium_findings.description: Verdict: NEEDS_REVIEW. Static command-execution findings are not embedded execution by the skill, but the documentation asks users to install packages and run twscrape CLI commands. Users should verify the upstream package and understand command effects before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are documentation examples and not automatic execution. Risk remains because installation and CLI commands execute third-party code and operate on account data.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Export of Scraped Personal Data
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. Example workflows write scraped user and tweet data to output files. This is expected for data workflows, but it can store personal data locally and may create retention or privacy obligations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are clear and intentional. The severity depends on what data the user collects and how the output path is chosen.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Static Ruby Backtick Command Matches
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The repeated external command detections come from Markdown code fences and inline backticks, not Ruby backtick execution. No evidence found that the skill automatically executes shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirms these locations are Markdown examples. The scanner appears to treat fenced code delimiters or inline command formatting as executable Ruby syntax.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Match
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The reported weak cryptography locations are ordinary prose about GraphQL and tweets. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or any cryptographic operation in these lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not contain cryptographic APIs or algorithms. This is a strong false-positive assessment based on direct line review.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Matches
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The reported reconnaissance lines refer to tweet IDs or user lookup examples, not host system enumeration. No evidence found of commands such as uname, whoami, env dumping, or network interface inspection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are application examples using tweet or user identifiers. They do not inspect the local machine or execution environment.

dangerous_patterns.title: Account Rotation Used for Rate Limit Management
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill promotes adding multiple accounts and rotating them when limits are reached. This is a dangerous pattern for a marketplace because it can enable large-scale scraping and platform limit evasion.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The rotation behavior is a named feature and is tied to rate-limit handling. The abuse risk is semantic, not just pattern-based.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive Account Material in Setup Flows
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples include passwords, email passwords, and cookie strings as inputs to account setup. This pattern can expose sensitive credentials if users paste real values into prompts, logs, or shared files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sensitive fields are explicit. The examples use placeholders, but the operational workflow requires real secrets.

dangerous_patterns.title: Proxy Credentials and SOCKS Proxy Strings
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation shows proxy URLs with embedded usernames and passwords, including SOCKS localhost examples. This can route scraping traffic through opaque infrastructure and expose proxy credentials.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The proxy patterns are concrete and repeated. Their risk depends on user intent and infrastructure controls.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent Local Account Database
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses accounts.db by default and supports custom database paths. Persistent account storage can contain sensitive session or credential material and needs strong local protection.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The database behavior is documented directly. The exact contents are inferred from account management examples, so confidence is high but not maximal.

content.value_statement: Teams need a practical way to collect public Twitter/X data for monitoring, research, and analysis. This skill explains twscrape workflows for searches, profiles, followers, trends, account pools, proxies, and exports.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to install twscrape from PyPI or the upstream GitHub repository.
Shows Python async examples for tweet search, user profiles, followers, replies, retweeters, and trends.
Documents account pool setup with cookies, credentials, login flows, and account status commands.
Covers proxy configuration through API options, account settings, and environment variables.
Provides examples for exporting collected tweets and user data to local files.
Summarizes rate-limit behavior, account rotation, and troubleshooting steps.
limitations: It documents a third-party library and does not guarantee Twitter/X access or stability.
It may conflict with platform terms, privacy rules, or local data protection requirements.
It requires user-managed accounts, cookies, proxies, and local credential storage.
It does not provide built-in consent, retention, or compliance controls.

use_cases.title: Monitor Public Conversations
use_cases.description: Track keywords, trends, replies, and high-engagement posts for public social listening workflows.

use_cases.title: Build Research Datasets
use_cases.description: Collect public tweets, user metadata, and follower networks for reproducible research with documented limits.

use_cases.title: Prototype Data Pipelines
use_cases.description: Test async collection, export formats, and account pool behavior before building a production data pipeline.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Search
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a simple keyword collection task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the safest basic twscrape workflow for collecting a small sample of public tweets for one keyword. Include setup steps and privacy cautions.

prompt_templates.title: Compare User Data Methods
prompt_templates.scenario: Choose the right API method for user-related collection.
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain which twscrape methods I should use to collect a public user profile, recent posts, replies, followers, and following lists. Note rate-limit concerns.

prompt_templates.title: Plan an Export Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Design a local output process for analysis.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a twscrape export workflow for tweets and user metadata. Include file naming, retention, and steps to avoid storing unnecessary personal data.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Scraping Pipeline
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review before running a larger collection job.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my planned twscrape pipeline for account rotation, proxies, error handling, rate limits, data minimization, and compliance risks before I run it.

output_examples.input: Collect recent public posts for one keyword.
output_examples.output: A short workflow with installation checks, account setup notes, a small search limit, expected fields, and warnings about platform policy and data retention.

output_examples.input: Analyze one public profile and its recent activity.
Prepare a dataset for a research project.
output_examples.output: A collection plan that defines scope, minimization rules, local storage location, export format, and review steps before sharing results.

best_practices: Use the smallest collection limit that answers the research or monitoring question.
Keep credentials, cookies, proxy secrets, and account databases outside prompts and shared repositories.
Review platform terms, consent expectations, and local privacy law before collecting or storing data.
anti_patterns: Do not use account rotation to evade platform limits or enforcement controls.
Do not paste real passwords, cookies, or proxy credentials into chat logs or example files.
Do not collect follower networks or exports without a clear purpose and retention plan.

faq.question: Does this skill run twscrape automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation and examples. Users still choose whether to install packages and run commands.

faq.question: Does it require Twitter/X credentials?
faq.answer: Many workflows require accounts, cookies, or credentials. Store these secrets securely and never share them in prompts.

faq.question: Can it access private data?
faq.answer: The documentation focuses on API and scraping workflows. Users are responsible for permissions, privacy rules, and platform terms.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk high?
faq.answer: The skill documents account rotation, proxies, credentials, cookies, and unofficial scraping. These patterns can support abuse if misused.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code, but users must manage execution and compliance.

faq.question: What should I check before using it?
faq.answer: Check platform terms, legal requirements, account security, data minimization, retention rules, and whether the official API is more appropriate.
